---
title: "Bayesian GAMs: R Comparison with brms"
author: "GAM.jl Contributors"
format:
  html:
    code-fold: false
    toc: true
  gfm:
    toc: true
engine: knitr
---

## Overview

This vignette fits the same Bayesian GAMs as `11_bayesian_gam.qmd` using R's **brms** package, which translates GAM formulas into Stan code and fits them via Hamiltonian Monte Carlo. We compare posterior distributions between GAM.jl (Turing.jl backend) and brms (Stan backend) using point estimates, posterior SDs, credible intervals, and empirical CDF comparisons.

Both approaches use the same underlying idea: the **smooth2random** decomposition converts smooth terms into random effects, and MCMC estimates the smoothing variance $\sigma_s^2$ alongside all other parameters.

## Setup

```{r message=FALSE}
library(brms)
library(mgcv)
library(ggplot2)
library(posterior)

# Read Julia posterior samples for comparison
julia_gauss <- read.csv("../posteriors_gaussian_julia.csv")
julia_poisson <- read.csv("../posteriors_poisson_julia.csv")
```

## Example 1: Gaussian GAM

### Data

```{r}
dat <- read.csv("../data_bayes_gaussian.csv")
cat(sprintf("n = %d, y range: [%.2f, %.2f]\n", nrow(dat), min(dat$y), max(dat$y)))
```

### Frequentist reference (mgcv)

```{r}
m_freq <- gam(y ~ s(x, k = 10), data = dat, method = "REML")
summary(m_freq)
```

### Bayesian fit (brms)

In brms, `s()` uses mgcv's basis construction internally. We set priors to match GAM.jl's `PriorSpec(sds = Exponential(1.0))`:

```{r cache=TRUE, results='hide', message=FALSE}
m_brms <- brm(
  y ~ s(x, k = 10),
  data = dat,
  family = gaussian(),
  prior = c(
    prior(normal(0, 10), class = "b"),
    prior(exponential(1), class = "sds"),
    prior(normal(0, 2.5), class = "sigma", lb = 0)
  ),
  chains = 2,
  iter = 2000,
  warmup = 1000,
  seed = 2024,
  backend = "rstan"
)
```

### Posterior summary

```{r}
summary(m_brms)
```

### Extract posterior samples

```{r}
draws <- as_draws_df(m_brms)

# Residual SD
sigma_brms <- draws$sigma
cat(sprintf("brms Ïƒ: mean = %.4f, sd = %.4f, median = %.4f\n",
            mean(sigma_brms), sd(sigma_brms), median(sigma_brms)))
cat(sprintf("  95%% CI: [%.4f, %.4f]\n",
            quantile(sigma_brms, 0.025), quantile(sigma_brms, 0.975)))
cat(sprintf("Frequentist Ïƒ: %.4f\n", sqrt(m_freq$scale)))

# Smooth SD
sds_cols <- grep("^sds_", names(draws), value = TRUE)
if (length(sds_cols) > 0) {
  sds_brms <- draws[[sds_cols[1]]]
  cat(sprintf("\nbrms sds: mean = %.4f, sd = %.4f\n",
              mean(sds_brms), sd(sds_brms)))
}

# Intercept
b_int <- draws$b_Intercept
cat(sprintf("\nbrms intercept: mean = %.4f, sd = %.4f\n",
            mean(b_int), sd(b_int)))
cat(sprintf("Frequentist intercept: %.4f\n", coef(m_freq)[1]))
```

### Comparing brms and GAM.jl posteriors

#### Point estimates and SDs

```{r}
cat("Parameter         | brms (Stan)       | GAM.jl (Turing)   | Frequentist\n")
cat("------------------|-------------------|--------------------|------------\n")
cat(sprintf("Ïƒ_obs  mean       | %.4f             | %.4f              | %.4f\n",
            mean(sigma_brms), mean(julia_gauss$sigma_obs), sqrt(m_freq$scale)))
cat(sprintf("Ïƒ_obs  sd         | %.4f             | %.4f              |\n",
            sd(sigma_brms), sd(julia_gauss$sigma_obs)))
cat(sprintf("Intercept mean    | %.4f            | %.4f             | %.4f\n",
            mean(b_int), mean(julia_gauss$beta_intercept), coef(m_freq)[1]))
cat(sprintf("Intercept sd      | %.4f             | %.4f              |\n",
            sd(b_int), sd(julia_gauss$beta_intercept)))
if (length(sds_cols) > 0) {
  cat(sprintf("Ïƒ_s mean          | %.4f             | %.4f              |\n",
              mean(sds_brms), mean(julia_gauss$sigma_s1)))
  cat(sprintf("Ïƒ_s sd            | %.4f             | %.4f              |\n",
              sd(sds_brms), sd(julia_gauss$sigma_s1)))
}
```

#### Empirical CDF comparison

The **Kolmogorov-Smirnov test** provides a formal comparison of the posterior distributions from brms and GAM.jl:

```{r}
# Ïƒ_obs ECDF comparison
ks_sigma <- ks.test(sigma_brms, julia_gauss$sigma_obs)
cat(sprintf("KS test for Ïƒ_obs: D = %.4f, p = %.4f\n",
            ks_sigma$statistic, ks_sigma$p.value))

# Intercept ECDF comparison
ks_int <- ks.test(b_int, julia_gauss$beta_intercept)
cat(sprintf("KS test for intercept: D = %.4f, p = %.4f\n",
            ks_int$statistic, ks_int$p.value))

# Ïƒ_s ECDF comparison
if (length(sds_cols) > 0) {
  ks_sds <- ks.test(sds_brms, julia_gauss$sigma_s1)
  cat(sprintf("KS test for Ïƒ_s: D = %.4f, p = %.4f\n",
              ks_sds$statistic, ks_sds$p.value))
}
```

Note: A high p-value (> 0.05) indicates the posteriors are not significantly different.

#### ECDF plots

```{r fig.width=10, fig.height=4}
par(mfrow = c(1, 3))

# Ïƒ_obs ECDF
plot(ecdf(sigma_brms), col = "blue", main = expression(sigma[obs]),
     xlab = expression(sigma), ylab = "ECDF", lwd = 2)
lines(ecdf(julia_gauss$sigma_obs), col = "red", lwd = 2)
abline(v = sqrt(m_freq$scale), lty = 2, col = "black")
legend("bottomright", c("brms", "GAM.jl", "Frequentist"),
       col = c("blue", "red", "black"), lty = c(1, 1, 2), lwd = 2, cex = 0.8)

# Intercept ECDF
plot(ecdf(b_int), col = "blue", main = "Intercept",
     xlab = expression(beta[0]), ylab = "ECDF", lwd = 2)
lines(ecdf(julia_gauss$beta_intercept), col = "red", lwd = 2)
abline(v = coef(m_freq)[1], lty = 2, col = "black")
legend("bottomright", c("brms", "GAM.jl", "Frequentist"),
       col = c("blue", "red", "black"), lty = c(1, 1, 2), lwd = 2, cex = 0.8)

# Ïƒ_s ECDF
if (length(sds_cols) > 0) {
  plot(ecdf(sds_brms), col = "blue", main = expression(sigma[s]),
       xlab = expression(sigma[s]), ylab = "ECDF", lwd = 2)
  lines(ecdf(julia_gauss$sigma_s1), col = "red", lwd = 2)
  legend("bottomright", c("brms", "GAM.jl"),
         col = c("blue", "red"), lty = 1, lwd = 2, cex = 0.8)
}
```

#### Quantile-quantile comparison

```{r fig.width=10, fig.height=4}
par(mfrow = c(1, 3))
probs <- seq(0.01, 0.99, by = 0.01)

# Ïƒ_obs Q-Q
q_brms <- quantile(sigma_brms, probs)
q_julia <- quantile(julia_gauss$sigma_obs, probs)
plot(q_brms, q_julia, pch = 16, cex = 0.5,
     main = expression(sigma[obs] ~ "Q-Q"),
     xlab = "brms quantiles", ylab = "GAM.jl quantiles")
abline(0, 1, col = "red", lwd = 2)

# Intercept Q-Q
q_brms_int <- quantile(b_int, probs)
q_julia_int <- quantile(julia_gauss$beta_intercept, probs)
plot(q_brms_int, q_julia_int, pch = 16, cex = 0.5,
     main = "Intercept Q-Q",
     xlab = "brms quantiles", ylab = "GAM.jl quantiles")
abline(0, 1, col = "red", lwd = 2)

# Ïƒ_s Q-Q
if (length(sds_cols) > 0) {
  q_brms_sds <- quantile(sds_brms, probs)
  q_julia_sds <- quantile(julia_gauss$sigma_s1, probs)
  plot(q_brms_sds, q_julia_sds, pch = 16, cex = 0.5,
       main = expression(sigma[s] ~ "Q-Q"),
       xlab = "brms quantiles", ylab = "GAM.jl quantiles")
  abline(0, 1, col = "red", lwd = 2)
}
```

## Example 2: Poisson GAM

### Frequentist reference

```{r}
dat2 <- read.csv("../data_bayes_poisson.csv")
m_freq2 <- gam(y ~ s(x, k = 10), data = dat2, family = poisson(), method = "REML")
cat(sprintf("Frequentist intercept (log-scale): %.4f (true: 1.0)\n", coef(m_freq2)[1]))
```

### brms fit

```{r cache=TRUE, results='hide', message=FALSE}
m_brms2 <- brm(
  y ~ s(x, k = 10),
  data = dat2,
  family = poisson(),
  prior = c(
    prior(normal(0, 10), class = "b"),
    prior(exponential(1), class = "sds")
  ),
  chains = 2,
  iter = 2000,
  warmup = 1000,
  seed = 2024,
  backend = "rstan"
)
```

### Posterior summary

```{r}
summary(m_brms2)
```

### Comparing posteriors

```{r}
draws2 <- as_draws_df(m_brms2)
b_int2 <- draws2$b_Intercept
sds_cols2 <- grep("^sds_", names(draws2), value = TRUE)

cat("Parameter         | brms              | GAM.jl            | Freq     | True\n")
cat("------------------|-------------------|-------------------|----------|------\n")
cat(sprintf("Intercept mean    | %.4f            | %.4f            | %.4f   | 1.0\n",
            mean(b_int2), mean(julia_poisson$beta_intercept), coef(m_freq2)[1]))
cat(sprintf("Intercept sd      | %.4f             | %.4f             |          |\n",
            sd(b_int2), sd(julia_poisson$beta_intercept)))
if (length(sds_cols2) > 0) {
  sds2 <- draws2[[sds_cols2[1]]]
  cat(sprintf("Ïƒ_s mean          | %.4f             | %.4f             |          |\n",
              mean(sds2), mean(julia_poisson$sigma_s1)))
}
```

### KS tests

```{r}
ks_int2 <- ks.test(b_int2, julia_poisson$beta_intercept)
cat(sprintf("KS test for intercept: D = %.4f, p = %.4f\n",
            ks_int2$statistic, ks_int2$p.value))

if (length(sds_cols2) > 0) {
  ks_sds2 <- ks.test(sds2, julia_poisson$sigma_s1)
  cat(sprintf("KS test for Ïƒ_s: D = %.4f, p = %.4f\n",
              ks_sds2$statistic, ks_sds2$p.value))
}
```

### ECDF and Q-Q plots

```{r fig.width=8, fig.height=4}
par(mfrow = c(1, 2))

# Intercept ECDF
plot(ecdf(b_int2), col = "blue", main = "Poisson intercept ECDF",
     xlab = expression(beta[0]), ylab = "ECDF", lwd = 2)
lines(ecdf(julia_poisson$beta_intercept), col = "red", lwd = 2)
abline(v = 1.0, lty = 2, col = "black")
legend("bottomright", c("brms", "GAM.jl", "True"),
       col = c("blue", "red", "black"), lty = c(1, 1, 2), lwd = 2, cex = 0.8)

# Intercept Q-Q
q_brms2 <- quantile(b_int2, probs)
q_julia2 <- quantile(julia_poisson$beta_intercept, probs)
plot(q_brms2, q_julia2, pch = 16, cex = 0.5,
     main = "Poisson intercept Q-Q",
     xlab = "brms quantiles", ylab = "GAM.jl quantiles")
abline(0, 1, col = "red", lwd = 2)
```

## Syntax Comparison

| Feature | Julia (GAM.jl + Turing) | R (brms + Stan) |
|---------|------------------------|-----------------|
| Bayesian GAM | `gam(f, data; priors = PriorSpec(...))` | `brm(f, data, family, prior = ...)` |
| Smooth SD prior | `PriorSpec(sds = Exponential(1.0))` | `prior(exponential(1), class = "sds")` |
| Residual SD prior | `PriorSpec(sigma = ...)` | `prior(..., class = "sigma")` |
| Per-smooth prior | `PriorSpec(specific = Dict("sds_s(x2)" => ...))` | `prior(..., class = "sds", coef = "sx2_1")` |
| Posterior samples | `m.chains[Symbol("Ïƒ_obs")]` | `as_draws_df(m)$sigma` |
| Coefficient table | `coeftable(m)` | `summary(m)` |
| Credible intervals | `confint(m; level = 0.95)` | `posterior_summary(m)` |
| Custom model | `@model function ... end` (Turing.jl) | Stan code (auto-generated) |
| MCMC backend | Turing.jl (NUTS via AdvancedHMC) | Stan (NUTS via rstan/cmdstanr) |

### Key differences

1. **API design**: GAM.jl uses the same `gam()` function for both frequentist and Bayesian â€” priors trigger Bayesian dispatch. brms uses a separate `brm()` function.

2. **Prior specification**: GAM.jl uses a structured `PriorSpec` with class-level defaults + per-parameter overrides. brms uses a vector of `prior()` statements.

3. **Basis construction**: Both use the same mgcv-style smooth2random decomposition. brms delegates directly to `mgcv::smoothCon()` + `mgcv::smooth2random()`. GAM.jl implements these natively in Julia.

4. **Inference**: brms generates Stan code (compiled C++) for HMC. GAM.jl builds a Turing.jl `@model` (Julia-native AD + NUTS). Both use the No-U-Turn Sampler.

5. **Non-centered parameterization**: Both use $\mathbf{b} = \sigma_s \cdot \mathbf{z}$, $\mathbf{z} \sim N(0, I)$ for improved sampling geometry.
